title: Reared by His Father and His Aunts
summary: Interviewed by Joe Mosnier at his Columbia, South Carolina home on June 8, 2011, Justice Ernest Finney Jr. opens with his upbringing after his mother died when he was ten days old, raised jointly by his father and his aunts, his father a Virginia State University graduate and schoolteacher in Smithfield, Virginia, where Finney was born, a strict man who ruled the roost fairly.

title: Bags Packed Within 24 Hours
summary: Finney describes a childhood of constant moves across Virginia whenever his father got a better teaching offer, his father's wartime work at the Pentagon, then Morgan State University in Baltimore, and finally the call from Claflin College in Orangeburg to be its dean, which put him on a train to South Carolina amid friends' warnings he would be lynched.

title: They Treated Us as Family
summary: He recalls the warm welcome that disproved his friends' exaggerated stories, his job as a soda jerk at the college soda shop owned by the Weber family on the edge of Claflin and South Carolina State, the active Black business community, a Ku Klux Klan parade down the street, and membership in Trinity United Methodist Church.

title: A Path to Hitch His Star To
summary: Finney recounts enrolling at Claflin around 1948 without a formalized ambition, and how observing the standing of Black lawyers in Washington's Black community during the Morgan State years, among them the immaculately dressed fraternity president and national leader Belford V. Lawson Jr., showed him the path he wanted to follow.

title: About Four Students in the Class
topic: Law School and the Brown Decision
summary: He describes beginning law studies at the South Carolina State College of Law in the fall of 1952, the dedicated faculty and tiny classes that gave individual attention, the lawsuit by a South Carolina State student that helped desegregate the University of South Carolina, and the ecstatic reaction on campus to the Brown decision weeks before he took the bar in 1954.

title: Walking in the Front Door of the Courthouse
topic: A Young Black Attorney in the 1950s
summary: Finney explains the challenges facing a young Black attorney in mid-1950s South Carolina, taking clients to the courthouse to show them a Black lawyer could pull down the books and walk in the front door, and his lasting disappointment that the state closed the South Carolina State law school while North Carolina kept the comparable Central law school open and integrated.

title: My First Meeting at the Bar Was as a Waiter
topic: Teaching in Conway and the Bar Banquet
summary: He recalls teaching school in Conway for about six years and working a second job at the Ocean Forest Hotel, where, because Blacks were barred from the bar association's social events, he attended his first South Carolina Bar Association meeting around 1954 as a waiter, a story he later told as Chief Justice when he sat at the head table.

title: A Decision to Go Full-Time
summary: Finney describes staying abreast of South Carolina's Black legal community and how a chance encounter with Matthew J. Perry Jr. and I. DeQuincey Newman, on their way to work on desegregating a state park, prompted his decision to move his family to Sumter and practice law full-time, helped by a part-time post teaching South Carolina history at Morris College, and remembers Newman, the NAACP field secretary, as active in everything in the civil rights community.

title: Jail Without Bail
summary: He recounts being called from Rock Hill to represent students from Friendship Junior College connected with the Congress of Racial Equality, whose field secretary James T. McCain was a fellow Sumter citizen, his car breaking down on the first trip, and his shock when, after the conviction, the students announced the new jail-no-bail tactic, with SNCC also active and one athlete student withdrawing for fear of losing his scholarship.

title: A Cadre of Good Lawyers
topic: The Volume of Civil Rights Work
summary: Finney explains that a lawyer articulating the Black community's concerns had to be everywhere at once, that he identified most closely with the active Sumter NAACP chapter, and that he and a cadre of lawyers mostly trained at South Carolina State took on financially insignificant cases to make a living, joking with Matthew Perry that even a Greyhound bus wreck out front would send the lucrative cases to established firms.

title: Beat Three Times but Never Lost Faith
topic: Civil Rights Commission and Running for Office
summary: He describes joining the South Carolina Advisory Committee to the US Commission on Civil Rights in 1963 as another avenue into the struggle, naming the active White Citizens' Council and the white ally James McBride Dabbs of the human rights effort, and notes that he ran for political office three times and lost three times before finally winning.

title: It Did Not Reach the Most Needy
summary: Asked about evolving race relations through the 1960s, Finney voices disappointment that the progress made did not benefit the neediest of the Black community, a current rather than ancient problem, pointing to the gap between Spartanburg County schools and the so-called Corridor of Shame, and the continuing need to give poor children a quality education.

title: I Resigned in Protest
summary: He recalls the 1968 Orangeburg Massacre shootings and how, when the next administration refused to reopen an investigation, he and Ben Payton and several others resigned, feeling the federal government had not used all its powers, noting he was chairman of the South Carolina committee of the US Commission on Civil Rights at the time.

title: Twenty-Five Percent Was All I Needed
topic: Winning the State House and the Black Caucus
summary: Finney describes putting together his 1972 State House race on little money by talking to anyone who would have him, the journalist Jack Bass noting in a 1975 interview that he drew 20 to 25 percent of the white vote, and his founding of the legislative Black Caucus on the conviction that the caucus first had to learn the rules to be a mechanism for change.

title: A Miracle Still Happened
summary: He recounts becoming a force in the legislature, then his 1985 election to the State Supreme Court with an inside track in the 124-member House against an opponent who worked the Senate, and his 1994 election as Chief Justice by the legislature, which left him shocked and pleased in the belief that hard work earns its share of the vote.

title: My Most Famous Run
summary: Prompted by his wife, Finney recalls the most famous race he lost, a run for Congress for an open seat against a sitting congressman he believes was Albert W. Watson, drawing perhaps 1,800 votes, and tells of declining overtures from Robert F. Kennedy to join the Justice Department in Washington, preferring to stay and work in South Carolina, which had been good to him.

title: Almost as Bad as Separate but Equal
summary: Finney discusses Abbeville County School District v. State of South Carolina, a case in which poor school districts sought more financial support, and his effort to keep the case alive so legislative leaders might resolve the tax-base disparity between wealthy and poor counties, the ruling he caught the most flack for likening to separate but equal, and his later work with Richard W. Riley against school voucher proposals.
related_events: Abbeville County School District v. State of South Carolina

title: I Had Taken This Oath
summary: Finney closes on the hardest decision of his career: though unalterably opposed to the death penalty as a legislator, he was assigned as a circuit judge to try a man who had robbed and raped, and after a Horry County jury fixed the sentence at death by electrocution he had to impose it, calling it a hard day's work under the oath he had taken, before thanking his hosts.
